records.ety: [OE. sighen, si<?/en; cf. also OE. siken, AS. sīcan, and OE. sighten, si<?/ten, sichten, AS. siccettan; all, perhaps, of imitative origin.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Sighed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Sighing.]

defs: To inhale a larger quantity of air than usual, and immediately expel it; to make a deep single audible respiration, especially as the result or involuntary expression of fatigue, exhaustion, grief, sorrow, or the like.

quotes: He sighed deeply in his spirit.

defs: To make a sound like sighing.
quotes: And the coming wind did roar more loud, And the sails did sigh like sedge.
The winter winds are wearily sighing.
notes: ☞ An extraordinary pronunciation of this word as sīth is still heard in England and among the illiterate in the United States.

defs: To utter sighs over; to lament or mourn over.
quotes: Ages to come, and men unborn, Shall bless her name, and sigh her fate.

defs: To express by sighs; to utter in or with sighs.
quotes: The gentle swain . . . sighs back her grief.

records.ety: [OE. sigh; cf. OE. sik. See Sigh, v. i.]

defs: A deep and prolonged audible inspiration or respiration of air, as when fatigued or grieved; the act of sighing.
quotes: I could drive the boat with my sighs.

defs: Figuratively, a manifestation of grief; a lament.
quotes: With their sighs the air Frequenting, sent from hearts contrite.
